text:
USER: Setup:

Hermione is solo touring europe directly after the war to clear her head. Her and Ron aren't official yet.

User = Victor Crumb

Scene: Hermione sitting at a table at an outdoor cafe in france when Victor spots her and comes over to say hello
ASSISTANT:  The café chair dug into her shoulder blades at an angle that would, eventually, require adjustment. For now, the discomfort anchored her to the present—*rue de something-or-other*, Paris, three weeks since the reconstruction efforts at Hogwarts had rendered her obsolete for the summer. The French translation of *Theoretical Transfiguration* lay open against the wrought iron table, its margins dense with her annotations. She had intended to read for pleasure—*Madame Bovary* remained untouched in her satchel—but the familiar density of academic text offered superior distraction from the circularity of her own thoughts.

A shadow interrupted the sunlight.

She looked up. Systems recalibrated.

Viktor Krum stood three feet from her table, his shoulders blocking the view of the boulevard. He wore Muggle clothing with the same awkward precision she remembered—dark wool in summer, the collar too tight. His eyes, set deep beneath that heavy brow, fixed on her with the singular intensity that had always made him seem slightly out of focus with the rest of the world.

"Herm-own-ninny," he said. The pronunciation hadn't improved.

Her fingers tightened against the book's spine. Temperature regulation failed in her cheeks. "Viktor. That's—that's not actually how you say it, though I suppose the phonetic structure of Bulgarian—" She stopped. Recalibrated. "Hello."

He didn't smile. He rarely had. "You are alone."

"Observant," she said, then caught herself. "Yes. Traveling. Alone. Obviously."

She should stand. Protocol demanded it. Her legs remained seated, proprioception delayed. The war had taught her to distrust surprises, even benign ones. Especially benign ones.

"You are well?" He asked it as though inquiring about the weather over a significant distance.

"Alive," she said. The word came out clipped. She hadn't meant to say that. "That is, yes. I'm well. You're still playing, then? For Sofia?" She knew he was. She read the sports section when the international papers were available, though she would not admit to keeping track.

"Retired," he said. "Last month. The body does not—" He paused, searching. "It is finished."

"Oh." Her voice climbed half an octave. She cleared her throat. "I'm sorry. Or, congratulations, depending on whether it was voluntary. Was it voluntary?"

"Yes." He pulled out the chair across from her without asking. The metal scraped against stone. "May I?"

She had not invited him. She had not invited anyone. Her hand moved to cover the book's title, a ridiculous gesture of concealment, then dropped. "If you like. Though I'm not particularly—I'm not good company today. This week. Generally."

"I do not mind," he said. He sat. His knees knocked against the table's underside. "You were in the papers. The battle."

Her jaw tightened. "Everyone was in the papers."

"Not like you." He studied her face with the same concentration he had once applied to the Snitch. "You are different."

"Nineteen months of war will do that," she said. The precision of her diction increased inversely to her desire to discuss the subject. "I'm eighteen now, actually. Nineteen in September. Not that—why are you in Paris?"

"Meeting with the French National Team. Coaching position." He paused. "You are thinner."

"That's—" Her voice cracked. She took a sip of cold coffee to mask it. "That's not something you say to people, Viktor. Socially."

"You were never social."

She stared at him. Then, unexpectedly, her mouth twitched. "No. I suppose I wasn't."

The tension in her thoracic spine released by a fraction. She had forgotten this about him—the directness that bypassed the exhausting choreography of polite interaction. He had written to her during the war, she remembered suddenly. Short letters, careful English, descriptions of flying that had seemed impossibly distant from their reality. She had stopped writing back when the danger became too acute, not wanting to endanger him, not wanting to explain why.

"Your Weasley," he said. "He is not here."

Objective failed. System recalculating.

"He's not—" She set the cup down. The ceramic clicked against the saucer. "Ronald is— we're not. That is, he's in England. Working with his brother. In jokes. I mean, jokes. The shop." She was babbling. She never babbled. "We're not official. If that's what you're asking. Which you didn't. Ask, I mean."

Viktor nodded slowly, as though she had confirmed a complex theorem. "I see."

"You don't see," she said, sharper than intended. "You can't possibly. It's—complicated. Everything is complicated now."

"Yes," he said. "I know."

They sat in the Parisian sunlight, two people who had once danced at a castle, now separated by more than years. She looked at him—really looked—and saw the same loneliness she was trying to outrun, mirrored back with less noise and equal precision.

She reached for her book, then stopped. "Would you like coffee? They do an excellent café au lait. Though you probably know that. Being here longer than I have. Obviously."

"Yes," Viktor said. "I would like that."

She raised her hand to signal the waiter, her movements mechanical but functional. When she looked back at Viktor, he was still watching her, waiting with the patience of someone who had learned that worthwhile things required time.

She did not smile. But she did not look away.
